title: MySQL Intro
content:
MySQL Introduction

The Database Standard

MySQL is a widely used open-source relational database management system (RDBMS). It is fast, reliable, scalable, and works on almost all platforms.

Unlike NoSQL databases (like Firebase) which store data in flexible documents, MySQL strictly stores data in structured tables with rows and columns. You interact with this data using SQL (Structured Query Language).

title: What is RDBMS?
content:
Relational Database Systems

Tables and Keys

An RDBMS organizes data into strictly defined tables. Each table represents an entity (like Users, Recipes, or Orders). Tables are connected to one another by "Relationships" using unique Primary Keys and Foreign Keys.

title: SQL Basics
content:
SQL Basics

The Language of Data

SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language used to communicate with relational databases. You use SQL commands to define data structures, and to manipulate the data inside them.

  • DDL (Data Definition): CREATE, ALTER, DROP (Used to build tables).
  • DML (Data Manipulation): SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE (Used to handle the actual data).

title: Create Tables
content:
Creating Databases & Tables

Setting up the Schema

Before you can save data, you must create a database and define the strict structure (schema) of your tables.

DDL Commands
-- Create the main database
CREATE DATABASE MealMitraDB;

-- Select the database to use
USE MealMitraDB;

-- Create a table with strict columns
CREATE TABLE users (
  id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  city VARCHAR(50)
);

title: Insert / Update
content:
Insert, Update, Delete

Manipulating Records

Once your table structure is ready, you can add new records (rows) using INSERT, modify existing rows using UPDATE, and remove them completely using DELETE.

Modifying Data
-- Adding a new user
INSERT INTO users (name, city) 
VALUES ('Sanjith', 'Chennai');

-- Updating an existing user (Always use a WHERE clause!)
UPDATE users 
SET city = 'Bangalore' 
WHERE id = 1;

-- Deleting a user
DELETE FROM users WHERE id = 2;

title: SELECT Queries
content:
SELECT Queries

Reading Data

The SELECT statement is the most frequently used command in SQL. It fetches data from your tables and returns it as a result set. Use the asterisk * to select all columns, or specify exact columns to save bandwidth.

Fetching Data
-- Fetch absolutely everything from the users table
SELECT * FROM users;

-- Fetch only the names, and sort them alphabetically
SELECT name 
FROM users 
ORDER BY name ASC;

title: Filtering
content:
Filtering and Conditions

The WHERE Clause

You rarely want to pull an entire database at once. Use WHERE, AND, OR, and NOT to filter query results precisely.

Conditional Filters
-- Find all users located in a specific city
SELECT * FROM users 
WHERE city = 'Chennai';

-- Find users whose name contains the letter 'a'
SELECT * FROM users 
WHERE name LIKE '%a%';

title: Joins
content:
Joins in MySQL

Connecting Tables

A JOIN combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column between them (like connecting a User ID to a Meal Plan ID). This is the true power of a relational database.



INNER JOIN Example
-- Fetch the username and their assigned meal plan
SELECT users.name, meal_plans.plan_title
FROM users
INNER JOIN meal_plans 
ON users.id = meal_plans.user_id;

title: Aggregate Functions
content:
Aggregate Functions

Database Math

Aggregate functions perform calculations on a set of values and return a single, summarized value. They are essential for building dashboards and analytics.

Common Aggregates
-- Get the total number of registered users
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users;

-- Find the highest calorie count in the recipes table
SELECT MAX(calories) FROM recipes;

-- Calculate the average cost of all meal plans
SELECT AVG(price) FROM meal_plans;

title: Group By
content:
Group By and Having

Organizing Aggregates

The GROUP BY statement groups rows that have the same values into summary rows (like finding the number of users in each city). Because you cannot use the WHERE clause on aggregate functions, SQL uses the HAVING clause to filter grouped records.

Grouping Data
-- Count how many users live in each city, 
-- but ONLY show cities with more than 10 users!

SELECT city, COUNT(*) 
FROM users 
GROUP BY city 
HAVING COUNT(*) > 10;

title: Aliases & Wildcards
content:
Aliases and Wildcards

Search and Format

Aliases (AS) are used to give a table or a column a temporary name, making results easier to read. Wildcards are used with the LIKE operator to search for specific patterns in text.

Formatting Results
-- Rename the output column dynamically
SELECT name AS username FROM users;

-- Find any recipe that starts with 'Chicken' 
-- The % symbol represents zero, one, or multiple characters
SELECT * FROM recipes 
WHERE title LIKE 'Chicken%';

title: Indexes
content:
Indexes and Performance

Speeding Up Queries

When a table gets massive, searching through it row-by-row (a full table scan) takes too long. Indexes are special data structures created on specific columns to drastically speed up data retrieval.



Creating an Index
-- If we frequently search for users by city, we should index it!
CREATE INDEX idx_city ON users (city);

-- Now, this query will run infinitely faster on large tables:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE city = 'Chennai';

title: Views
content:
Database Views

Virtual Tables

A View is a virtual table based on the result-set of an SQL statement. It always shows up-to-date data. They are incredibly useful for hiding complex, massive JOIN queries behind a simple, easy-to-query virtual table.

Creating a View
-- Save a complex query as a View
CREATE VIEW active_subscribers AS 
SELECT name, email 
FROM users 
WHERE subscription_status = 'Active';

-- Now you can query the View just like a normal table!
SELECT * FROM active_subscribers;

title: Functions
content:
Functions and Built-ins

Database Utilities

MySQL comes packed with built-in string, numeric, and date functions. It is almost always faster to have the database format this data before sending it to your backend (like PHP or Python).

Built-in Functions
-- Combine strings together
SELECT CONCAT(firstName, ' ', lastName) AS fullName FROM users;

-- Get the current date and time
SELECT NOW();

-- Format dates into readable strings
SELECT DATE_FORMAT(created_at, '%Y-%m-%d') FROM users;

title: Advanced Queries
content:
Advanced Queries

Complex Data Retrieval

Advanced SQL queries involve stacking operations together. You can combine multiple result sets using UNION, or use Subqueries (nested SELECT statements) to use the result of one query as the filter for another.

Using CASE
-- Using CASE to create dynamic columns based on logic
SELECT name, calories, 
  CASE 
    WHEN calories < 300 THEN 'Healthy'
    WHEN calories BETWEEN 300 AND 600 THEN 'Moderate'
    ELSE 'Heavy'
  END AS diet_category
FROM recipes;

title: MySQL Summary
content:
MySQL Final Summary 🚀

Data Master! 🏆

Congratulations! You have completed the comprehensive MySQL curriculum. Understanding relational databases gives you a massive advantage in software architecture.

  • Architecture: Structuring Databases and Tables with strict Data Types.
  • CRUD Operations: Inserting, Reading, Updating, and Deleting records.
  • Relationships: Linking data powerfully using Foreign Keys and JOINs.
  • Optimization: Leveraging Views and Indexes for massive performance boosts.

Whether you are using Firebase for mobile apps, or MySQL for traditional web backends, your understanding of data flow is now rock solid!

title: LIMIT Clause
content:
MySQL LIMIT Clause

Pagination

When building an app, you never want to load 10,000 records at once—it will crash the user's phone! The LIMIT clause restricts the number of records returned. By combining it with OFFSET, you can build seamless pagination (like loading 10 recipes at a time).

Limiting Results
-- Fetch the top 5 highest calorie recipes
SELECT * FROM recipes 
ORDER BY calories DESC 
LIMIT 5;

-- Pagination: Skip the first 20 records, then fetch the next 10
SELECT * FROM users 
ORDER BY id ASC 
LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20;

title: Wildcards
content:
Wildcards with LIKE

Pattern Matching

Wildcards are used with the LIKE operator to search for specific patterns in strings. They are the foundation of basic search bars.

  • % : Represents zero, one, or multiple characters.
  • _ (underscore) : Represents a single, exact character.
Search Patterns
-- Finds any user whose email ends with '@gmail.com'
SELECT * FROM users WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com';

-- Finds any recipe starting with 'C' and exactly 4 letters long (e.g., Cake)
SELECT * FROM recipes WHERE title LIKE 'C___';

title: Advanced Operators
content:
Advanced Operators

Complex Logic

MySQL supports advanced logical operators like IN, BETWEEN, and NOT to make your queries significantly cleaner and easier to read.

IN and BETWEEN
-- Instead of: WHERE city = 'Chennai' OR city = 'Delhi' OR city = 'Mumbai'
SELECT * FROM users 
WHERE city IN ('Chennai', 'Delhi', 'Mumbai');

-- Find recipes with calories between 300 and 600
SELECT * FROM recipes 
WHERE calories BETWEEN 300 AND 600;

title: Built-in Functions
content:
Built-In Functions

Data Formatting

MySQL has dozens of built-in functions for manipulating data right at the database level before it ever reaches your backend code.

  • String: CONCAT(), SUBSTR(), UPPER(), LOWER()
  • Numeric: ABS(), ROUND(), CEIL(), FLOOR()
  • Date: NOW(), DATEDIFF(), DATE_ADD()
Function Examples
-- Calculate how many days ago a user registered
SELECT name, DATEDIFF(NOW(), created_at) AS days_active 
FROM users;

-- Round recipe prices to 2 decimal places
SELECT title, ROUND(price, 2) FROM recipes;

title: Index Management
content:
Managing Indexes

The Performance Trade-off

While Indexes massively speed up SELECT searches, they come with a hidden cost: they slow down INSERT and UPDATE operations. This is because the database has to update the invisible index structure every time the data changes. You must balance where you apply them!



CREATE and DROP
-- Create an index on the email column for fast logins
CREATE INDEX idx_email ON users (email);

-- If a column is no longer searched frequently, drop the index to save memory
ALTER TABLE users DROP INDEX idx_email;

title: Views Deep Dive
content:
Views Deep Dive

Virtual Tables

A view is a virtual table based on the result of a SELECT query. They don't store data themselves; they pull data dynamically every time they are queried. They are heavily used to restrict access (hiding sensitive columns from certain users) and simplify massive JOIN operations.

Reusable Logic
-- Create a view that hides passwords and sensitive data
CREATE VIEW public_user_profiles AS 
SELECT id, name, city 
FROM users;

-- Query the view safely
SELECT * FROM public_user_profiles WHERE city = 'Chennai';

title: Aliases (AS)
content:
Aliases (AS)

Renaming on the Fly

Use the AS keyword to temporarily rename columns or entire tables during a query. This is especially useful when joining tables that have columns with the exact same name (like two tables that both have an `id` column).

Table & Column Aliases
-- 'u' is a Table Alias. 'user_name' is a Column Alias.
SELECT u.name AS user_name, p.title AS meal_plan_title
FROM users AS u
JOIN meal_plans AS p 
ON u.id = p.user_id;

title: Next Steps
content:
Career & Certification

Proving Your Skills

Database management is one of the most highly sought-after skills in tech. By mastering MySQL, you have laid the foundation for data engineering, backend development, and system architecture.



To prove your skills to employers, consider taking an official online MySQL certification exam. Certifications test your knowledge of SQL queries, functional design, and database schema architecture, making your resume stand out instantly!

title: Advanced Summary
content:
MySQL Advanced Summary 🚀

Database Expert! 🏆

Congratulations! You have completed the advanced MySQL curriculum. You now have the tools required to build highly scalable and performant databases.

  • Data Formatting: Utilized Built-in String, Date, and Numeric Functions.
  • Query Optimization: Handled large datasets cleanly with LIMIT, OFFSET, and Aliases.
  • Architecture: Mastered the trade-offs of Index creation and utilized Views for security.
  • Complex Logic: Mapped data patterns with Wildcards, IN, and BETWEEN operators.

Your backend knowledge is fully solidified. On to the next great technology!

title: MySQL Triggers
content:
MySQL Triggers

Event-Driven SQL

A trigger is a special stored program in MySQL that automatically executes (or "fires") when certain events occur on a specific table. Common events include INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE.



They fire automatically in response to database changes without needing your backend code (like Python or PHP) to manually call them, making them perfect for enforcing absolute data integrity at the lowest level.

title: Trigger Types
content:
Types of Triggers

Before and After

Triggers in MySQL are executed relative to table events. You can choose to run your custom logic exactly before the data is saved, or immediately after.

  • BEFORE INSERT / AFTER INSERT: Runs when a new row is added.
  • BEFORE UPDATE / AFTER UPDATE: Runs when an existing row is modified.
  • BEFORE DELETE / AFTER DELETE: Runs when a row is removed.

title: Creating a Trigger
content:
Creating a Trigger

The Syntax

Use the CREATE TRIGGER statement to define a trigger. Inside the trigger body, you use the NEW and OLD keywords to access the incoming data and the existing data, respectively.

Automatic Timestamps
-- This trigger fires automatically right before a user is updated
CREATE TRIGGER update_timestamp
BEFORE UPDATE ON users
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
  -- Sets the updated_at column to the exact current time
  SET NEW.updated_at = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;
END;

title: Use Cases
content:
Use Cases for Triggers

Database Automation

Triggers handle database automation tasks, ensuring your backend server doesn't have to carry all the burden of validation and logging.



  • Audit Logs: Automatically copying a deleted user's data into a 'deleted_users_history' table right before they are permanently removed.
  • Data Validation: Preventing an INSERT if a user's age is calculated to be below zero.
  • Cascading Updates: Automatically recalculating a Meal Plan's total calorie count whenever a new Recipe is added to it.

title: Triggers Summary
content:
MySQL Triggers Summary 🚀

Automation Mastered! 🏆

Triggers provide a way to run SQL code automatically in response to table events. They are a powerful feature for maintaining data integrity, enforcing strict business rules, and automating database tasks without relying on manual intervention from your backend codebase.

With Triggers added to your skillset, you can build self-sustaining, bulletproof databases!

title: Stored Procedures
content:
Stored Procedures

Reusable Database Logic

A stored procedure is a prepared block of SQL code that you can save and reuse directly inside the database engine. Once defined, you can call a stored procedure multiple times from your backend without having to rewrite or transmit the raw SQL statements over the network.



Stored procedures help modularize database logic, improve query reuse, and drastically reduce network traffic between your application and your database.

title: CREATE PROCEDURE
content:
Creating a Stored Procedure

The Syntax

Use the CREATE PROCEDURE command to define a stored procedure. The actual SQL logic you want to execute is wrapped inside the BEGIN and END keywords.

Defining the Procedure
-- This creates a procedure that fetches all users
CREATE PROCEDURE SelectAllUsers()
BEGIN
  SELECT * FROM users;
END;

title: Executing Procedures
content:
Calling a Stored Procedure

The CALL Command

To run a stored procedure after creating it, you use the CALL command. When your backend (like Python, PHP, or Node.js) talks to MySQL, it only has to send this one tiny command instead of a massive multi-line query!

Executing the Code
-- This triggers the code block we defined earlier
CALL SelectAllUsers();

title: Parameters
content:
Procedure Parameters

Passing Data In

Stored procedures become incredibly powerful when they accept parameters. You can use IN (pass a value to the procedure), OUT (pass a value back), or INOUT. This allows you to build highly flexible, dynamic queries.

Using IN Parameters
-- Create a procedure that requires a user ID to run
CREATE PROCEDURE GetUserById(IN targetId INT)
BEGIN
  SELECT * FROM users 
  WHERE id = targetId;
END;

-- Call the procedure and pass in the number 5
CALL GetUserById(5);

title: Advantages
content:
Advantages of Stored Procedures

Why Use Them?

For enterprise applications like a meal-planning architecture, moving logic to stored procedures has massive benefits:



  • Speed: The database compiles and caches the query execution plan, making subsequent calls lightning fast.
  • Security: You can grant a user permission to CALL a procedure without giving them permission to SELECT or DELETE the underlying tables directly.
  • Maintainability: If the database structure changes, you only update the procedure in one place, rather than updating queries in 10 different backend microservices.

title: DROP PROCEDURE
content:
Deleting Stored Procedures

Clean Up

If you make a mistake or no longer need a stored procedure, you can remove it entirely using the DROP PROCEDURE statement.

Dropping Procedures
-- Deletes the procedure from the database memory
DROP PROCEDURE SelectAllUsers;

-- Best practice: check if it exists first to avoid errors
DROP PROCEDURE IF EXISTS GetUserById;

title: Final Summary
content:
Stored Procedures Summary 🚀

Database Logic Mastered! 🏆

Congratulations! You have completed the ultimate MySQL curriculum. You have learned how to shift complex application logic straight into the database engine itself.

  • Creation: Building reusable blocks of code with CREATE PROCEDURE.
  • Execution: Using CALL to trigger logic from a remote backend.
  • Dynamic Data: Passing IN and OUT parameters for flexible queries.
  • Optimization: Understanding the performance and security benefits of pre-compiled database code.

Your MySQL knowledge is now fully complete and production-ready!
